nativeRange.habitat: Coastal to outer reef lagoons over rubble and hard coral, generally reported around 5–35 m depth
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical Indo-West-Pacific reef rubble and patch reefs

diet.notes: Generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty foods (krill, clam, squid), and high-quality marine flake or pellets. Tends to do best with several small feedings per day given its active swimming style.

avoidWith: other male fairy wrasses in the same tank

imageCandidates.notes: LMBM2012 (English Wikipedia); 3264x2448. Tack-sharp full-body aquarium shot showing all diagnostic features: orange-yellow face, red dorsal edge, green-blue flank, white belly, violet tail.
desertnaturalist (iNaturalist); 1324x1070. Vivid in-situ nuptial male, red head with red eye-ring, orange-red back, bright blue belly and flowing fins; sharp, full body, adds color-form variety.
Rickard Zerpe (Flickr); 2308x1731. Sharp in-situ male over bubble coral, red head and eye-ring, warm iridescent flank, blue ventral fins and tail; different angle/individual.

summary: A peaceful Indo-Pacific fairy wrasse with a blue body, red dorsal edge, and orange-yellow face; generally considered reef-safe and one of the more beginner-friendly fairy wrasses.
careNotes: The Red Head Solon Fairy Wrasse (Cirrhilabrus solorensis) is found across the Western Pacific from Indonesia south to northern Australia, where it inhabits coastal and outer reef rubble and patch-reef habitats at depths of roughly 5–35 m. Most individuals reach around 10–12 cm in aquariums, though some sources place the upper end slightly higher. Like all fairy wrasses in the genus Cirrhilabrus, this species is generally regarded as reef-safe and is not known to harm corals or ornamental shrimp. It tends to be a bold, active swimmer and does best with plenty of open water to move through, along with live rock for shelter and resting spots. One important precaution: the Red Head Solon Fairy Wrasse is a capable jumper, and a tightly fitted lid or eurobrace (a rimless-tank cover strip) is strongly recommended. Individuals have also been reported to sleep wedged among rockwork inside a self-secreted mucous cocoon — a normal behavior shared by many wrasses. This species is protogynous, meaning individuals are born female and can transition to male later in life. Males display more vivid coloration and will generally be aggressive toward other males kept in the same system. The recommended arrangement is a single male with one or more females, with all females introduced simultaneously if possible to reduce conflict. Some taxonomic sources still treat this fish as a color form of Cirrhilabrus cyanopleura (the Blueside Wrasse), though the aquarium trade generally lists it as a distinct species.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species page used for water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), 90 gal min tank, easy care level, peaceful temperament, reef-safe rating, carnivore diet, and trade synonyms. Reached via the wrasse-reef-safe collection landing page given in the manifest.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4.5 in ≈ 11.4 cm), peaceful temperament with tendency to be bullied, reef-safe status, carnivore diet, and 50 gal min tank. Tank-size disagreement with LiveAquaria's 90 gal noted; final 300 L picked per playbook tank-size rule biasing toward the larger value for an active swimmer.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed max length ~11 cm TL, family Labridae, distribution Western Pacific (Indonesia to northern Australia), depth ~5–35 m, IUCN Data Deficient.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked 400 L minimum tank, ~26°C target, SG ~1.026, jumping/escape behavior, sleep-in-mucous-cocoon trait, and male conspecific aggression in confined space.
